straight_beat.name: Walt Kieniewicz
straight_beat.column:
The Cubs lost again. That’s what the box score says. Yesterday’s game against the Pirates was another instance of the team’s inability to capitalize on opportunities, another reminder that the 2023 season is shaping up to be a slog through the NL Central. The Pirates, for all their mediocrity, found a way to win, and the Cubs, for all their potential, found a way to squander it.  

The Pirates’ 4-3 victory wasn’t a masterpiece, but it was efficient. They didn’t need heroics. Their starter, a guy named Cole, didn’t allow a run in six innings. Their bullpen, which has been a liability all year, held the fort. And their offense, which has been a question mark, scratched out enough hits to win. The Cubs, on the other hand, had a chance to take the lead in the seventh inning. They didn’t. That’s what the box score says.

optimist.name: Beatrice 'Bee' Vittorini
optimist.column:
The Cubs lost yesterday, but let’s not dwell on the scoreboard. Let’s linger on the way Crow-Armstrong dove for that ball, arms flailing like a startled raccoon, and somehow came up with it. That’s the kind of hustle that makes you believe in the ivy climbing Wrigley’s walls—tenacious, stubborn, always reaching for the light. You don’t need a win to feel hope; you just need moments like that, flickers of something bigger than the box score.  

I’ve been tending flowers at Lincoln Square for years, and I’ve learned that beauty grows in the most unexpected places. The Cubs’ season is no different. Sure, they’re 6-8, three games back in the NL Central, but let’s not forget the roots beneath the surface. This team is still young, still finding its rhythm, and every loss is a lesson in how to play. Yesterday’s game? A reminder that even when the numbers

pessimist.name: Tony Gedeski
pessimist.column:
The Cubs lost again. Pittsburgh’s 4-3 victory yesterday wasn’t just another loss—it was a mirror held up to a team that’s been staggering through the season like a drunk man trying to dance. Six wins, eight losses, and a 3.0-game gap in the NL Central. You could almost hear the front office whispering, “Ricketts gonna Ricketts,” as they handed out contracts to stars and ignored the rot in the bullpen.  

Let’s talk about that bullpen. It’s not just bad; it’s a carnival ride. You spend a billion on the marquee and forget to build a bullpen. Every Cubs summer is the same jazz and the same hangover. The starters are okay, sure, but once they hand the ball to the relievers, it’s like watching a car crash in slow motion. Javy Guerra’s fastball looks like a toddler’s crayon drawing. Why did they sign him? To make us feel better about the rest of the roster? The Pirates, for all their mediocrity, have a bullpen that can throw a curveball. Maybe they’re just better at managing their money.  

And let’s not forget the manager. You’d think with a .500 record, he’d have some new tricks up his sleeve. But no, it’s the same old playbook: hit home runs, hope for the best, and pray the bullpen doesn’t collapse. The offense is fine, but fine isn’t good enough. You can’t hit 10 home runs and still be 3 games out. The Pirates’ offense is barely better than the Cubs’, yet they’re laughing all the way to the bank. How? Because they didn’t waste their money on overpaid stars and instead built a team that actually functions.  

The front office keeps talking about the “vision” and “long-term plan,” but all I see is a bunch of executives in a boardroom, sipping
